old_claim.note: Merchant Courier Networks provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Pilgrimage services operate along physical route corridors; the Santiago evidence identifies routes, bridges, roadside crosses, and route sections as part of the pilgrimage infrastructure.

source_shape.source_locator: Outstanding Universal Value says medieval pilgrims followed four routes through France; churches, hospitals, bridges, roadside crosses, and route sections illustrate pilgrimage conditions.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: UNESCO frames the Santiago property as medieval pilgrimage routes marked by route sections, bridges, hospitals, churches, and roadside crosses.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports roads and route corridors as the physical substrate for pilgrimage route services. It does not support making merchant courier systems a prerequisite for pilgrimage services.

ontology_before: Pilgrimage Route Services depended on Merchant Courier Networks, implying pilgrim services emerged from private merchant communications infrastructure.
ontology_after: Pilgrimage Route Services depends on roads, preserving the physical route-corridor substrate while keeping merchant couriers scoped to commercial messaging.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: pilgrimage_route_services now depends on roads and no longer directly depends on merchant_courier_networks.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to merchant messenger logistics for pilgrimage businesses rather than pilgrim route services.
A source showed medieval pilgrimage services directly required private merchant courier networks.
The roads node were narrowed so it no longer covered constructed paths and road systems for moving people.
short_reason: Pilgrimage route services require route infrastructure, not merchant courier systems.
